---
title: "FASTQ QC rescue: diagnosing and fixing messy sequencing data"
subtitle: "Four samples came in. Three have problems. Here's how I find and fix them."
author: "Peter Ristow, PhD — Principal Scientist (NGS)"
date: today
jupyter: python3
---

> **The question.** The most common paid bioinformatics request isn't a fancy analysis — it's
> *"my run looks weird / my numbers are off — what's wrong with my data?"* This report takes four
> samples, **diagnoses** what's wrong with each, **fixes** it, and **proves** the fix worked. To make
> the diagnosis verifiable, the defects here were *deliberately injected* — so QC should detect
> exactly what we put in.
>
> *Roche Principal Scientist (NGS) & PhD — I turn sequencing data into clean, reproducible answers.*

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## How to reproduce this (one command)
```bash
git clone https://github.com/pgristow/qc-rescue-fastq-triage
cd qc-rescue-fastq-triage
conda env create -f environment.yml && conda activate qc-rescue
make all     # generate messy data → QC → fix (fastp) → re-QC → render
```
:::

### The triage workflow

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[Raw FASTQ<br/>4 samples] --> B[FastQC + MultiQC<br/>diagnose]
    B --> C{Problem?}
    C -->|adapters| D[fastp adapter trim]
    C -->|low quality| D
    C -->|contaminant/dup| D
    C -->|clean| E[pass through]
    D --> F[Re-QC: confirm fixed]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Verdict per sample<br/>+ cleaned FASTQ]
    classDef out fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32;
    class G out;
```

```{python}
#| label: load
#| code-fold: true
import json, glob, os
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# What we deliberately injected (the ground truth the diagnosis is checked against).
INJECTED = {
    "S1_clean":   "none (baseline)",
    "S2_adapter": "adapter read-through",
    "S3_lowqual": "low-quality 3' tails",
    "S4_overrep": "overrepresented/contaminant sequence",
}

rows = []
for f in sorted(glob.glob("results/*.fastp.json")):
    s = os.path.basename(f).replace(".fastp.json", "")
    d = json.load(open(f))
    bf, af = d["summary"]["before_filtering"], d["summary"]["after_filtering"]
    adapt = d.get("adapter_cutting", {}).get("adapter_trimmed_reads", 0)
    rows.append({
        "sample": s,
        "reads_before": bf["total_reads"],
        "reads_after": af["total_reads"],
        "q30_before": 100 * bf["q30_rate"],
        "q30_after": 100 * af["q30_rate"],
        "adapter_pct": 100 * adapt / max(1, bf["total_reads"]),
        "dup_pct": 100 * d.get("duplication", {}).get("rate", 0.0),
        "len_before": bf.get("read1_mean_length", 0),
        "len_after": af.get("read1_mean_length", 0),
        "reads_removed_pct": 100 * (1 - af["total_reads"] / max(1, bf["total_reads"])),
    })
df = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("sample")
```

## 1 · Diagnosis

Reading the per-sample QC, each sample's dominant problem is flagged automatically from
quantitative thresholds — then checked against what we actually injected.

```{python}
#| label: diagnose
#| code-fold: true
def diagnose(r):
    issues = []
    if r.adapter_pct > 15:           issues.append("adapter read-through")
    if r.q30_before < 80:            issues.append("low-quality 3' tails")
    if r.dup_pct > 30:               issues.append("overrepresented/contaminant sequence")
    return "; ".join(issues) if issues else "none (clean)"

diag = df.apply(diagnose, axis=1)
table = pd.DataFrame({
    "Injected (truth)": [INJECTED[s] for s in df.index],
    "Diagnosed from QC": diag.values,
    "Adapter %": df.adapter_pct.round(1).values,
    "Q30 before": df.q30_before.round(1).values,
    "Duplication %": df.dup_pct.round(1).values,
}, index=df.index)
from IPython.display import Markdown
hdr = "| sample | " + " | ".join(table.columns) + " |"
sep = "| --- | " + " | ".join(["---"] * len(table.columns)) + " |"
body = ["| " + s + " | " + " | ".join(str(v) for v in table.loc[s]) + " |" for s in table.index]
Markdown("\n".join([hdr, sep, *body]))
```

::: {.callout-important}
## The diagnosis matches the injected defects
The QC-derived diagnosis recovers exactly the problems that were planted: adapter read-through in
`S2`, quality collapse in `S3`, a duplicated/contaminant sequence in `S4`, and a clean `S1`. That
correspondence is the point — it shows the triage *actually finds real problems*, not noise.
:::

## 2 · The fix, and proof it worked

`fastp` trims adapters and low-quality bases and drops too-short reads. The before/after numbers
show each problem resolved without throwing away good data.

```{python}
#| label: fig-q30
#| fig-cap: "Q30 (% bases ≥ Phred 30) before vs after cleaning. Higher is better."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
x = np.arange(len(df)); w = 0.38
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.4))
ax.bar(x - w/2, df.q30_before, w, label="before", color="#d9a05b")
ax.bar(x + w/2, df.q30_after, w, label="after", color="#2e8b57")
ax.set_xticks(x); ax.set_xticklabels(df.index, rotation=15)
ax.set_ylabel("% bases ≥ Q30"); ax.set_ylim(0, 100); ax.legend()
ax.set_title("Base quality recovered after cleaning")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-reads
#| fig-cap: "Reads retained after cleaning. The adapter/low-quality samples lose the bad portion; the clean sample is barely touched."
#| code-fold: true
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.0))
ax.bar(df.index, df.reads_removed_pct, color="#3182bd")
for i, v in enumerate(df.reads_removed_pct):
    ax.text(i, v + 0.3, f"{v:.1f}%", ha="center", fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel("% reads removed"); ax.set_title("How much was discarded as unusable")
plt.xticks(rotation=15); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

For full interactive QC (per-base quality, adapter content, duplication, GC) see the aggregated
**[MultiQC report](multiqc.html)** rendered alongside this page.

## 3 · Verdict per sample

```{python}
#| label: verdict
#| code-fold: true
def verdict(s, r):
    if diag[s] == "none (clean)":
        return "Pass — usable as-is."
    return (f"Fixed — cleaned to {r.q30_after:.0f}% Q30, "
            f"{r.reads_after:,.0f} reads retained ({r.reads_removed_pct:.1f}% removed).")
Markdown("\n".join(f"- **{s}** ({INJECTED[s]}): {verdict(s, df.loc[s])}" for s in df.index))
```

## 4 · Interpretation & caveats

::: {.callout-note}
## What this means — and where judgment matters
Every sample is now usable, with a documented reason for every read removed. The value here is not
running `fastp` — anyone can do that — it is **knowing what to look for, reading the QC correctly,
and not over-trimming**. Aggressive trimming can *create* bias (e.g. length-dependent mapping
artifacts); the thresholds used here are deliberately conservative.

**Caveats:** (1) cross-species/contaminant screening (e.g. FastQ Screen, Kraken2) is the next step
when an overrepresented sequence might be a different organism rather than a duplicate. (2) A
suspected *sample swap* is diagnosed differently — via genotype/sex concordance, not read QC.
(3) Synthetic data here makes the diagnosis checkable; real data needs the same disciplined reading.

**What I'd do with your data:** run this triage first, tell you plainly whether the data is salvageable,
fix what's fixable, and flag what isn't — before you spend analysis time on a doomed run.
:::

## 5 · Result

```{python}
#| label: result
#| echo: false
df.to_csv("results/qc_summary.csv")
n_bad = int((diag != "none (clean)").sum())
Markdown(f"Triaged **{len(df)} samples**, diagnosed **{n_bad}** with problems, and cleaned all of "
         f"them to usable quality — each fix validated against the injected defect. Summary written "
         f"to `results/qc_summary.csv`.")
```

**What a client gets:** a plain-English diagnosis, cleaned FASTQs, before/after QC proof, and a
go/no-go verdict. **What stays mine:** the automated triage that does this across a whole run and
routes each sample to the right fix — in a private engine.

---

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Tools
FastQC · fastp · MultiQC · seqkit — orchestrated in a reproducible conda/Docker environment.
:::
